title: 10 best herbal teas for chickenpox
intro_desc: Herbal teas for chickenpox are infusions made from various dried herbs, flowers, and roots, which can help alleviate symptoms of chickenpox such as fever, itching, and irritability. Chamomile tea, for instance, has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help reduce inflammation and promote relaxation. Licorice root tea can help soothe a sore throat and boost the immune system, while peppermint tea can help relieve itching and reduce fever. By drinking these herbal teas, individuals with chickenpox can experience improved sleep, reduced discomfort, and faster healing. These teas offer a natural and gentle alternative to over-the-counter medications, making them an excellent addition to any chickenpox treatment plan.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Marshmallow teas helps with chickenpox because of its soothing properties that ease itching, inflammation, and discomfort. The mucilage in marshmallow root forms a protective barrier on the skin, reducing redness and irritation caused by the itchy blisters. Additionally, marshmallow's anti-inflammatory compounds help to calm the body's response to the virus, reducing fever and discomfort associated with chickenpox. Drinking marshmallow tea can also provide relief from respiratory symptoms like coughing and congestion that often accompany the illness.
remedy_properties: Demulcent property: Marshmallow tea's demulcent property helps soothe and protect the skin, reducing inflammation and discomfort associated with chickenpox blisters.
Anti-inflammatory property: The anti-inflammatory compounds in marshmallow tea may help reduce redness, swelling, and pain caused by chickenpox rashes.
Antioxidant property: Marshmallow tea's antioxidants can help combat oxidative stress and free radical damage caused by the virus, which can accelerate healing and reduce the risk of complications.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are rich in mucilage, a thick, protective substance that helps soothe and calm irritated skin.
Bark: The bark contains high amounts of saponins, which have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce itching and inflammation caused by chickenpox.
remedy_recipe: Harvest althaea officinalis roots and cut them into small pieces weighing approximately 20-25 grams per cup.
Combine the root pieces with boiling water in a pot to create an infusion, allowing it to steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a large bowl, discarding solids and reserving liquid.
Measure out 1 cup of cooled tea and mix it with 20-25 grams of sugar or honey dissolved in a small amount of hot water.
Transfer the sweetened tea to a clean glass jar and store it in the refrigerator for up to 3 days before serving chilled.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Pot marigold teas helps with chickenpox because it has been traditionally used to reduce fever, itching, and inflammation associated with this common childhood illness. The tea's anti-inflammatory properties can help soothe the skin and relieve the uncomfortable symptoms of chickenpox, such as blisters and rashes. Additionally, pot marigold's antimicrobial properties may also help prevent infection and promote healing in affected areas.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Pot marigold tea contains flavonoids and carotenoids, which help reduce inflammation and relieve symptoms such as itching, redness, and swelling associated with chickenpox.

Antiviral activity: The tea's saponins and phenolic acids have been shown to exhibit antiviral properties, which may help combat the varicella-zoster virus that causes chickenpox and reduce the severity of symptoms.

Wound-healing compounds: Pot marigold tea contains antioxidants and flavonoids that promote wound healing by improving blood circulation, reducing scarring, and preventing infection, which can aid in the recovery process for chickenpox sores.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds that help soothe itchy skin and reduce swelling associated with chickenpox.
Flowers: Containing calendic acid, a natural antiseptic and anti-inflammatory compound that helps prevent infection and reduce fever.
remedy_recipe: Harvest calendula flowers from your garden or purchase dried calendula petals from a reputable source.
Measure one teaspoon of dried calendula petals or three teaspoons of fresh petals per cup of boiling water.
Combine the measured calendula petals with one cup of boiling water in a tea infuser or heat-resistant cup.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the bioactive compounds to infuse into the hot water.
Strain the tea and discard the solids before serving hot or allowing it to cool to drink.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: German chamomile teas helps with chickenpox because it has natural anti-inflammatory properties that can soothe the itchy and painful rash associated with the virus. The tea's calming effects can also help to reduce stress and anxiety, which are common symptoms of chickenpox. Additionally, chamomile has antimicrobial properties that may aid in the body's natural defense against infection, promoting a faster recovery from the illness.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory property: Chamomile's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as apigenin and luteolin, can help reduce redness, swelling, and itching associated with chickenpox rashes.
Antiviral property: Chamomile contains flavonoids and terpenoids that have been shown to inhibit the replication of viruses, including those that cause chickenpox (varicella-zoster virus).
Soothing and calming property: Chamomile's natural sedative effects can help calm the nervous system, reducing stress, anxiety, and discomfort often experienced during a chickenpox outbreak.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in anti-inflammatory compounds that help soothe itchy skin and reduce inflammation associated with chickenpox.
Flowers: Contains apigenin, an antioxidant that calms the skin and reduces itching, redness, and swelling caused by chickenpox.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried chamomile flowers according to botanical standards, ideally during dawn or dusk for optimal potency.
Measure out one teaspoon of dried chamomile flowers per eight ounces of boiling water for every serving desired.
Bring the measured water to a boil and then reduce heat to a simmer allowing it to steep for 5-7 minutes with the chamomile flowers.
Strain the tea mixture using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup or teapot, discarding the solids and retaining the liquid gold.
Enjoy the warm soothing brew immediately, taking care to avoid consuming hot beverages within an hour of bedtime to promote restful slumber.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Horsetail teas helps with chickenpox because it is rich in antioxidants, vitamins, and minerals that boost the immune system and reduce inflammation. The tea's high silica content also helps to soothe and calm itchy skin, reducing the discomfort associated with the rash. Additionally, horsetail's antimicrobial properties can help combat secondary infections that often occur during chickenpox outbreaks.
remedy_properties: Antiviral properties: Horsetail tea has been shown to have antiviral activity against various viruses, including herpes simplex virus (which causes chickenpox), which may help reduce the severity and duration of the outbreak.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The flavonoids present in horsetail tea have anti-inflammatory effects, which can help soothe itchy skin, reduce swelling, and alleviate discomfort associated with chickenpox rash and blisters.

Wound-healing properties: Horsetail tea's high silica content may aid in promoting wound healing by improving collagen synthesis, strengthening connective tissue, and enhancing the overall structure of the skin, which can help speed up the recovery process from chickenpox-induced lesions.
remedy_parts: Roots: Horsetail roots contain high levels of silica, which helps to reduce inflammation and itching associated with chickenpox.
Bark: The bark of the horsetail plant has anti-inflammatory properties, making it effective in soothing skin irritations caused by chickenpox.
remedy_recipe: Gather fresh or dried equisetum arvense herb in amounts of one teaspoon for every cup of water.
Boil water in a pot and pour it over the horsetail herb in a heat-resistant cup or teapot.
Allow the mixture to steep for 10-15 minutes to release the herbal properties and flavors.
Strain the tea into another cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove solids.
Drink the tea immediately after straining while still warm, ideally within 30-60 minutes of preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Meadowsweet teas helps with chickenpox because its anti-inflammatory properties soothe itchy rashes and reduce swelling, making it a popular remedy for this common childhood illness. The tea's natural antiseptic qualities also help to prevent secondary infections that can occur when the skin is broken or scratched. Additionally, meadowsweet contains salicylic acid, a compound similar to aspirin, which has been shown to reduce fever and relieve discomfort associated with chickenpox.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Meadowsweet tea contains salicylic acid, which has anti-inflammatory effects that can help reduce the itching, redness, and swelling associated with chickenpox.

Antiviral properties: The flavonoids present in meadowsweet tea have been shown to exhibit antiviral activity against certain viruses, including herpes simplex virus (which is often linked to chickenpox), helping to combat the infection and reduce its severity.

Pain-relieving compounds: Meadowsweet tea contains compounds such as salicin and meadowsweet saponins that have analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help alleviate the discomfort, pain, and fever associated with chickenpox.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in salicylic acid, which helps to reduce fever and inflammation associated with chickenpox.
Bark: Contains salicin, a compound similar to aspirin, which can help alleviate headache and joint pain symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried meadowsweet herb per eight ounces of boiling water.
Steep the mixture for seven to ten minutes or until desired strength is achieved.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup or teapot.
Add honey to taste if desired and stir well before serving immediately.
Prepare this herbal tea daily as needed or store it in an airtight container for up to three days.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Licorice teas helps with chickenpox because it contains glycyrrhizin, a compound that has anti-inflammatory properties. This allows it to soothe the itchy and irritated skin caused by the virus, providing relief from the uncomfortable itching and scratching that can lead to infection and further complications. Additionally, licorice tea's antiviral properties may also help to boost the body's natural defense against the chickenpox virus, reducing the severity and duration of the outbreak.
remedy_properties: Glycyrrhizin: This compound has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce fever, itching, and inflammation associated with chickenpox blisters.

Flavonoids: The flavonoids present in licorice tea have antiviral properties, which can help combat the varicella-zoster virus that causes chickenpox, reducing the severity of symptoms and duration of illness.

Saponins: Saponins in licorice tea have demulcent properties, soothing and calming irritated skin, mucous membranes, and digestive tract, providing relief from chickenpox-related itching, burning, and discomfort.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots have anti-inflammatory properties, which help to soothe and calm itchy skin associated with chickenpox.
Bark: The bark contains triterpenoids that help to reduce fever and alleviate symptoms of chickenpox.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried glycyrrhiza glabra roots in the morning for optimal flavor and aroma.
Clean and chop 2-3 grams of dried roots or 4-6 grams of fresh roots into small pieces.
Combine the chopped roots with 250 milliliters of boiling water and let steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve to remove solids and enjoy.
Store any leftover tea in an airtight container and refrigerate for up to three days.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Humulus lupulus teas helps with chickenpox because it has anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties that can alleviate symptoms such as fever, itching, and soreness. The tea's flavonoids and terpenes work together to reduce inflammation and modulate the immune response, which can help speed up the recovery process and minimize discomfort associated with chickenpox. Additionally, humulus lupulus has been traditionally used to soothe skin irritations and promote relaxation, making it a natural remedy for alleviating the distressing symptoms of this common childhood illness.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties: Humulus lupulus tea helps reduce inflammation, redness, and itching associated with chickenpox, while also preventing infection and promoting wound healing.

Antiviral properties: The herb's antiviral compounds have been shown to inhibit the replication of the varicella-zoster virus (VZV), which causes chickenpox, reducing the severity and duration of the illness.

Sedative and anxiolytic properties: Humulus lupulus tea's calming effects can help alleviate the anxiety, stress, and restlessness often associated with chickenpox, promoting a more comfortable and peaceful recovery.
remedy_parts: Roots: Known for their anti-inflammatory properties, Humulus lupulus roots help soothe and calm skin irritations associated with chickenpox.
Seeds: Rich in antioxidants and flavonoids, Humulus lupulus seeds provide a natural boost to the immune system, aiding in the body's defense against viral infections like chickenpox.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality humulus lupulus leaves and flowers at dawn or just before sunset for optimal potency and flavor.
Rinse the harvested materials gently with cold water to remove impurities and debris from the plant material.
Steep one teaspoon of dried humulus lupulus herb or two teaspoons of fresh herb in boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to separate the liquid from the solids.
Allow the tea to cool and then store it in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3 days before consumption.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: St John's wort teas helps with chickenpox because of its anti-inflammatory properties, which can reduce the severity of symptoms such as fever, headaches, and body aches. The tea's flavonoids also have antiviral effects, which may help to shorten the duration of the illness by inhibiting the replication of the virus. Additionally, St John's wort has been traditionally used to calm irritated skin and reduce itching, making it a soothing remedy for the characteristic rash associated with chickenpox.
remedy_properties: Antiviral activity: St. John's Wort has been shown to exhibit antiviral properties, which can help combat the varicella-zoster virus that causes chickenpox and reduce its severity.
Inflammation reduction: The herb contains flavonoids and terpenoids that may help reduce inflammation and itching associated with chickenpox, making it more comfortable for patients.
Antioxidant activity: St. John's Wort is rich in antioxidants, which can help protect the body from oxidative stress caused by the virus and support overall immune function during an infection.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are rich in saponins, which have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties that can help soothe and calm skin irritations associated with chickenpox.
Leaves: The leaves contain flavonoids and terpenoids that possess antiviral activity, making them effective against the herpes virus that causes chickenpox.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality st john's wort leaves and flowers from a trusted source.
Combine one teaspoon of dried st john's wort leaves with eight ounces of boiling water.
Steep the mixture for 10-15 minutes to allow the active compounds to infuse.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth and discard solids.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before consumption and drink within 24 hours.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: English lavender teas helps with chickenpox because of its soothing and calming properties, which can help to reduce the stress and discomfort associated with this common childhood illness. The tea's anti-inflammatory compounds can also help to ease itching and redness, while its antimicrobial properties may even aid in reducing the duration and severity of the infection. Additionally, lavender's calming effects on the nervous system can promote relaxation and restful sleep, which is essential for helping the body fight off the virus.
remedy_properties: Antiviral properties: Lavender tea has antiviral compounds that can help reduce the severity and duration of chickenpox symptoms, such as fever and rash.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The flavonoids and terpenes present in lavender tea have anti-inflammatory effects, which can help alleviate itching, redness, and swelling associated with chickenpox blisters.

Calming and relaxing properties: Lavender's calming essence can promote relaxation and reduce anxiety, making it easier for children (and adults!) to tolerate the discomfort and stress of having chickenpox.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of English lavender are used in herbal medicine due to their ability to reduce inflammation and soothe itching, making them effective in treating the painful rashes associated with chickenpox.

Seeds: Lavender seeds have antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties that help to combat bacterial and viral infections, reducing the severity of symptoms like fever and discomfort during a chickenpox outbreak.
remedy_recipe: Harvest dried lavender buds from lavandula angustifolia plants or purchase them in store.
Measure out one teaspoon of dried lavender buds for every eight ounces of boiling water.
Heat filtered water to a boil and pour it over the measured lavender buds in a teapot.
Allow the mixture to steep for ten to fifteen minutes to release the fragrant essential oils.
Strain the tea into cups and sweeten with honey or sugar if desired, serving warm.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemon balm teas helps with chickenpox because of its soothing and calming properties. The antiviral compounds present in lemon balm, such as rosmarinic acid, can help reduce the severity and duration of symptoms like fever, headache, and fatigue. Additionally, the tea's anti-inflammatory properties can alleviate the discomfort and itching associated with chickenpox rash, promoting a faster recovery and reduced risk of complications.
remedy_properties: Antiviral properties: Lemon balm tea's antiviral properties can help to reduce the severity and duration of chickenpox symptoms, such as fever, itching, and rashes.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds in lemon balm tea may help to alleviate itching, redness, and swelling associated with chickenpox, making it more comfortable for patients.

Antioxidant properties: Lemon balm tea's antioxidant properties can help protect the skin from damage caused by free radicals, which can contribute to scarring and discoloration, promoting faster recovery.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in essential oils, lemon balm roots have anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties that can help soothe itchy skin and reduce fever associated with chickenpox.
Leaves: The leaves contain flavonoids, which can help to reduce inflammation, ease itching, and promote healing of blisters caused by chickenpox.
remedy_recipe: Measure out one tablespoon of dried lemon balm leaves or two tablespoons of fresh leaves for each eight ounce cup.
Heat one quart of water in a kettle or pot until it reaches a rolling boil.
Pour the boiling water over the measured lemon balm leaves and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a strainer or cheesecloth to remove the leaves.
Serve the tea hot or cold, sweetened with honey if desired, and enjoy.

supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat chickenpox with herbal teas is by consuming chamomile tea. Chamomile has anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe the itchiness and discomfort caused by chickenpox. Drinking peppermint tea can also provide relief as it has cooling and calming effects on the skin. Additionally, lemon balm tea can boost the immune system, helping the body fight off the virus. However, it's important to note that while herbal teas can help alleviate symptoms, they should not replace medical treatment. Always consult with a healthcare professional for proper diagnosis and treatment.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to chickenpox that are treated with herbal teas include measles and shingles. For measles, teas made from herbs such as baical skullcap, chamomile, and liquorice root can help soothe symptoms and boost the immune system. Meanwhile, for shingles, teas made from St. John's Wort, valerian root, and passionflower can help reduce pain and inflammation, as well as promote healing. However, it's important to note that while herbal teas can be helpful in managing symptoms, they should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment. Always consult with a healthcare provider for proper diagnosis and treatment.
